summary: This notebook implements a production-ready inference pipeline for the Jane Street Market Prediction competition by orchestrating a weighted ensemble of six pre-trained models. It demonstrates how to efficiently process real-time tabular market data using Polars, handle temporal lags, apply cyclic time encodings, and standardize features with precomputed statistics. The author shows that normalizing ensemble weights and clipping predictions within a fixed range stabilizes outputs for the competition's scoring metric. Readers should learn how to structure a multi-model inference server, manage lag-based feature alignment across dates, and combine heterogeneous model families without retraining.
competition_flow: Loads pre-trained models and dataset statistics, processes test and lag data with Polars (cyclic time encoding, lag joining, standardization, categorical mapping), generates predictions from each model, applies normalized weighted averaging, clips outputs to [-5, 5], and serves the final submission via Kaggle's inference server.
data_reading: Reads parquet files using Polars, converts to Pandas for model inference, and loads pre-trained models and dataset statistics via dill and pickle.
data_processing: Applies cyclic time encodings, joins test data with lagged features grouped by date_id and symbol_id, forward-fills missing values, standardizes continuous features using precomputed global means and standard deviations, maps categorical features to integer indices, and clips final predictions to [-5, 5].
features_engineering: Cyclic time features (sin/cos of time_id), lagged responder features, global z-score standardization, and custom integer mappings for sparse categorical features.
cv_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation (splitting scheme not shown, only checkpoint loading visible)
ensembling: Combines predictions from four model groups (NN, Starter ensemble, TabM, Ridge) using normalized weights via weighted averaging, followed by clipping predictions to [-5, 5].
insights: Combining heterogeneous model families with normalized weights improves prediction stability.
Cyclic time encoding and lag-based feature joining effectively capture temporal dependencies in financial market data.
Standardizing features with precomputed global statistics ensures consistent model inputs across train and test splits.
Clipping predictions to a fixed range prevents extreme outliers from degrading the competition scoring metric.
